nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving small streams and forest tributaries of the Congo Basin, with dense marginal vegetation, leaf litter, and tannin-stained blackwater near the riverbanks. Reported from the Chiloango River and widespread through the central Congo basin; possibly also from the Nyong drainage in Cameroon.

decorPreferences: densely planted margins (Anubias, Java fern, Cryptocoryne) and floating cover (Salvinia, Riccia, Limnobium) to dim the light
driftwood, branches, and Indian almond leaves to release tannins and create shelters
leaf litter substrate over a soft dark base to mimic blackwater habitat
low to no flow — strong currents are poorly tolerated
tight-fitting lid with a warm air gap above the surface — labyrinth organ requires access to warm humid air and the fish is a known jumper
multiple visual breaks (caves, roots, plant thickets) to defuse male-male aggression

diet.notes: Micropredator generally considered insectivorous-carnivorous in the wild, hunting small invertebrates among leaf litter and submerged vegetation. In captivity, tends to favour small live and frozen meaty foods — bloodworm, daphnia, brine shrimp, mysis, mosquito larvae, micro-crustaceans. Dried/pellet foods are often refused on arrival; some specimens can be coaxed onto high-quality crushed micro-pellets over time, but a varied frozen/live diet is usually needed for long-term health and conditioning for breeding.

goodWith: small peaceful surface dwellers (Congo tetras of compatible size, lampeyes, hatchetfish)
peaceful Corydoras and small Synodontis catfish
calm, similarly-sized West African characins and killifish
biotope-appropriate dwarf cichlids in larger setups (e.g. Pelvicachromis) provided cover is plentiful
avoidWith: boisterous or fin-nipping species (tiger barbs, larger danios) that harass slow-moving labyrinth fish
much larger predatory tankmates that can swallow or outcompete it
ornamental shrimp and very small fry — likely to be predated as natural prey items
multiple males in tanks below ~120 cm (intraspecific aggression, especially around spawning)
highly active mid-water schoolers that monopolise food before the slow bushfish can feed

imageCandidates.notes: Juan Carlos Palau Diaz; 720x389. Sharp, vibrant live specimen showing the diagnostic jewel-red/gold body with dark iridescent vertical bars and ornate serrated dorsal/anal fins.
Juan Carlos Palau Diaz; 1401x934. Highest-res, tack-sharp full-body side profile; iridescent green-gold scales and ornate finnage clear, banding more muted.
Frank M. Greco; 640x343. Different individual showing red-edged fins and vertical bars; slightly softer/hazier, kept as non-recommended extra for variety.

summary: A small, jewel-coloured West African anabantoid from Congo Basin blackwater streams. Slow-moving micropredator best kept in a dimly lit, heavily planted tank with floating cover and a tight lid; males can be territorial toward each other.
careNotes:
The Banded Bushfish (Microctenopoma ansorgii) is a small Anabantoid from family Anabantidae — closely related to the larger Leopard Bush Fish (Ctenopoma acutirostre) but distinct in size, social structure and breeding mode. It possesses a labyrinth organ and breathes atmospheric air, so a tightly fitting lid with a warm, humid air gap above the water surface is essential; cold air drawn over the labyrinth organ has been associated with respiratory infections. The species is also a capable jumper and routinely escapes loose-fitting covers.

In the wild it occupies slow-moving, tannin-stained streams of the Congo Basin with dense marginal vegetation, submerged roots and leaf litter. Aquaria should mirror these conditions: subdued lighting, generous floating plants (Salvinia, Riccia, Limnobium) and tall background planting, driftwood and root tangles, and Indian almond / catappa leaves to release tannins. A dark substrate with leaf litter brings out the fish's iridescent vertical bands and reduces stress. Flow should be gentle — strong currents are poorly tolerated.

Adult size typically ranges from around 5.4 cm up to about 8 cm TL, with most aquarium specimens settling around 6–7 cm. A 75–80 cm tank of around 80 L is generally considered adequate for a pair or trio; for a group containing more than one adult male, a substantially longer footprint (around 120 cm) with abundant cover is generally advised to allow each male to hold a small territory.

Water chemistry should be soft and slightly acidic to neutral (pH ~6.0–7.5, gH 5–20). Lifespan in the aquarium is typically reported in the range of 4–6 years.

Feeding can be a slow process. The species is generally considered a micropredator and will rarely accept dry foods on arrival; offering small frozen and live items (bloodworm, daphnia, mysis, brine shrimp, mosquito larvae) is usually necessary, with patience required to transition any individuals onto prepared diets. Because it feeds slowly and deliberately, fast or greedy tankmates can effectively starve it through competition.
breedingNotes: Unlike the closely related Leopard Bush Fish (Ctenopoma acutirostre), Microctenopoma ansorgii is a bubble-nester. Conditioned pairs build a loose surface bubblenest, often under a broad leaf or floating cover, in dimly lit, soft, slightly acidic water at the warmer end of its range. Spawns of up to around 600 eggs have been reported, hatching in roughly 24 hours, with the male typically tending the nest. Fry are very small and require infusoria-grade first foods (paramecia, vinegar eels, commercial fry powders) before graduating to baby brine shrimp and microworms after several days. Parents should be removed once the fry become free-swimming. Conditioning with copious live and frozen foods, and triggering with a moderate cool, slightly acidic water change to simulate the onset of the rainy season, is generally advised.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Microctenopoma ansorgii
primary.notes: Promoted to primary because the originally assigned primary (Aquarium Co-Op product page at https://www.aquariumcoop.com/products/banded-bushfish) returned HTTP 404. Provided adult size (~7 cm SL), temperature 20–27°C, pH 5.5–7.5, hardness 5–20 dH, minimum tank ~75×30×30 cm, native range (Congo Basin in Republic of Congo and DRC), bubblenest breeding (up to ~600 eggs hatching in ~24 h), male-male aggression notes, lid/jumping warning, and aquarium lifespan up to ~5 years.

additional.name: FishBase — Microctenopoma ansorgii
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (5.4 cm SL / 8.0 cm TL reported), temperature 26–28°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 5–19 dH, native range (Chiloango River and widespread central Congo basin; possibly Nyong in Cameroon), family Anabantidae, order Anabantiformes, freshwater benthopelagic habitat, facultative air-breathing, IUCN Least Concern. Trophic level ~3.2 supports omnivorous/insectivorous micropredator diet classification.

additional.name: Aqua Imports — Ornate Bushfish (Microctenopoma ansorgii)
additional.notes: Specialty FW importer profile. Cross-checked adult size (~5 cm), temperature ~25.5–28.9°C, pH 6.5–7.2, minimum tank ~15 US gal (~57 L), Congo Basin blackwater origin, classification as social micropredator that benefits from tannin-stained water (driftwood, botanicals), and diet of small frozen meaty foods (small bloodworm, enriched brine, mysis). Notes that the species is best kept in groups and rarely accepts dried foods, which informed the disagreement in compatibility.grouping (resolved as pair / small group with male caveat).

fish.breedingNotes: A bubble-nester, unlike the closely related Ctenopoma acutirostre. Spawns of up to around 600 eggs have been reported, hatching in approximately 24 hours under the male's care. Fry require infusoria-grade first foods before advancing to baby brine shrimp. Conditioning with live and frozen foods and triggering with a cool soft-water change to simulate the rainy season is typically advised.
